skill.name: docs-validator
skill.description: Validates documentation quality for the Logseq Template Graph project by checking coverage, links, Markdown structure, examples, consistency, and actionable fixes.
skill.summary: Audit Logseq Template Graph documentation for coverage, links, formatting, accuracy, and consistency.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Manual review shows the weak-crypto, path traversal, reconnaissance, and hardcoded URL matches are false positives from prose or sample report text. The remaining risk is legitimate but elevated because the skill instructs agents to run shell documentation scans, inspect workspace files, optionally test external URLs, and write reports.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Directed Shell Documentation Scans
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Bash workflows that use find, ls, grep, shell variables, and loops to inspect documentation and source directories. This is legitimate for documentation validation, but it gives the agent command execution paths over the workspace and should be reviewed before use on sensitive repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are explicit and repeated, but they are limited to read-oriented documentation inventory and checks. I found no evidence of destructive commands, secret access, or exfiltration intent.

medium_findings.title: Optional External URL Validation
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the agent to check whether external documentation links are accessible. This can create outbound network traffic to URLs found in a repository, but the reviewed file does not instruct sending secrets or contacting a suspicious collection endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is semantically present in the link-validation workflow. The risk is moderate because it depends on repository links and no credential-bearing request is described.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography matches occur in documentation prose, checklist text, or sample report content. No cryptographic API, password hashing, cipher selection, or implementation code is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows documentation guidance and sample output, not executable cryptographic code. The file contains one Markdown skill description and no crypto implementation.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Match Is a Relative Link Example
low_findings.description: The path traversal match is part of a suggested fix for a broken Markdown link. It shows a relative documentation path and is not used for file access or command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is inside sample validation output and contains a Markdown link target. There is no code path that accepts user input and resolves this path.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Matches Are Sample Report Data
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs appear in an example broken-link report that shows an old URL and a replacement URL. They are not configured endpoints and do not receive repository data or credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are embedded in illustrative output, not in executable code. The broader external link checking behavior remains a medium operational risk, but these specific URLs are not malicious indicators.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Match Is Documentation Inventory
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance-related match is part of documentation link and file-reference validation. The skill asks for inventory of documentation paths, not host fingerprinting or system discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited area discusses internal documentation links and section anchors. I found no commands for OS, process, network interface, user, or environment reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides Bash command examples for repository-wide documentation discovery and validation. These commands are useful but should be constrained to trusted workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands are directly present and may be executed by an agent. Their apparent purpose is documentation validation, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Link Checking Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs validation of external links when online. This can generate outbound requests based on repository content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: External URL validation is explicitly described. I found no evidence that the workflow sends secrets or uses a fixed collection server.

content.value_statement: Documentation can become incomplete, inconsistent, or outdated as projects change. This skill audits Markdown docs and returns prioritized fixes.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether expected module README files and guide sections are present.
Reviews Markdown structure, header hierarchy, list formatting, tables, and code block language labels.
Identifies internal and external links that need validation or repair.
Compares examples, paths, terminology, and status notes for consistency.
Produces prioritized documentation reports with issue locations and suggested fixes.
Tracks coverage metrics for modules, guides, commands, skills, agents, and hooks.
limitations: The guidance is tailored to the Logseq Template Graph project structure.
External link checks require network access and may be skipped in restricted environments.
Shell examples should be reviewed before running on private or very large repositories.
The skill cannot prove external facts are current without trusted source review.

use_cases.title: Prepare Release Documentation
use_cases.description: Run a full audit before release to find missing guides, stale status notes, and broken references.

use_cases.title: Improve Contributor Onboarding
use_cases.description: Check whether setup, contributing, and troubleshooting content is complete and easy to follow.

use_cases.title: Review Module Coverage
use_cases.description: Find modules that lack README files, examples, schema references, or clear usage instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Documentation Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the documentation in this repository. Focus on missing files, broken internal links, and the top three fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Check Link Health
prompt_templates.prompt: Check documentation links. Separate internal links from external links, explain each issue, and suggest corrected targets.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Module Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit module documentation coverage. List modules with README files, missing README files, weak examples, and priority order.

prompt_templates.title: Run Full Documentation Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a full documentation audit for coverage, accuracy, formatting, links, consistency, examples, and release readiness.

output: Overall score: good, with two high-priority fixes and five medium-priority improvements.
Missing module documentation should be created before release.
Several guide links need updated targets, and one status note appears outdated.

output_examples.input: Check for broken links in the docs.
output: Internal links: most targets resolve, but one setup link points to a missing page.
External links: one download link returns an error and needs replacement.
Suggested fixes include corrected relative paths and updated external targets.
Most modules have README coverage, but one large module is missing documentation.
Priority should be based on module size, user impact, and missing examples.
The report recommends creating the missing README before polishing lower-priority formatting issues.

best_practices: Run quick checks during routine edits and full audits before releases.
Review shell and network actions before allowing automated validation.
Convert findings into small, prioritized documentation tasks with owners.
anti_patterns: Do not treat sample scores or example reports as verified project results.
Do not run external link checks when repository URLs or browsing activity are sensitive.
Do not accept broad documentation rewrites without checking the specific evidence.

faq.question: What does this skill validate?
faq.answer: It validates documentation coverage, Markdown formatting, links, examples, terminology, and consistency for the Logseq Template Graph project.

faq.question: Can it fix documentation automatically?
faq.answer: It can suggest specific fixes and create reports. Direct edits should still be reviewed by a maintainer.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Network access is only needed for external link validation. Internal documentation checks can run without network access.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill may scan file names and optionally request external URLs found in documentation.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it specific to one project?
faq.answer: Yes. The checks reference the Logseq Template Graph structure, but many documentation review ideas are reusable.
